narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 49% chance that you might not pass the bar exam on your first try, which is a borderline risk our admissions process flags to ensure student success. Your profile includes being a full-time male Asian student with a low family income bracket, an undergraduate GPA of 3.10, an LSAT score of 27, and school deciles placing you in the 3rd percentile for Year 1 grades and 2nd for Year 3—both notably below typical students, where averages hover around the 5th or 6th decile and LSAT scores near 37. The model weighed these factors carefully, with your Year 3 school decile standing out as the single most influential element, strongly nudging the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure due to its low ranking compared to peers. Right behind it in influence was your LSAT score, which also pushed toward failure because it's well below the dataset average. Your Year 1 school decile ranked third in impact, similarly contributing to the risk for being in a lower tier than most. Other notable factors included your racial background as Asian, low family income, and undergraduate GPA, each adding to the failure prediction in descending order of influence, while your gender and full-time status had minimal sway. Overall, these academic performance indicators—especially the low deciles and LSAT—dominated the decision, painting a picture riskier than the typical applicant who tends to have stronger high school rankings, higher test scores, and more average family resources. It's tough to hear, but this isn't a judgment on your potential; it's the model highlighting patterns from past students where similar profiles correlated with bar struggles. Looking ahead, you could realistically boost your bar exam odds by targeting improvements in areas tied to these predictors, like intensive LSAT retakes if eligible to build test-taking stamina, or focused academic prep programs to mimic higher-decile performance habits. Seeking scholarships or support for low-income students might also help level the playing field indirectly. With dedicated bar-specific study strategies, many students in tough spots turn things around—consider tutoring or practice exams tailored to your full-time schedule. You're capable of rewriting this story; keep pushing forward.
